Re: Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our PiIe No. I-90-543
Dear Mr. Biondo:
This is in response to your Letter requesting advice on
behalf of carlsbad City Councilmember John Mamaux, concerning his
duties under the confl ict-of-interest 
provisi.ons of the Political
Reform Act (the rra"tn1.1/ 
Your letter requests follow up advice
to our advice letter to you of July 6, Lggo.z I 
(Biondo Advice
Letter, No. I-90-241.) 
Since your advice request seeks general
guidance with respect to a series of decisiong, \^/e are treating
your request as one for informal assistance-r/
OUESTTONS
1. May councilmember l,lamaux participate in a city council
decision to establish a community facilities 
district 
$/here the
decision may rnaterially inpact business entities related to the
councilmember' s employer?
l/ 
Government code sections 81ooo-91015. AlL statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unLess otherwise indicated. 
com-
nission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
sectj.on 18ooo, et seq.- A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 5 of the California code of Regul-ations-
2/ 
PLease note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that has already taken p1ace. In
addition, this letter j-s based on the facts presented to us' 
The
Comnission does not act as the finder of fact. 
(In re oqelsbv
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

i**""iiy

83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3). )

2- Does the "public generally'r exception apply to decisions
concerning the community facilities 
district 
s/here the decisions
may impact business entitj-es related to the councilnember,s
emp Ioyer?
3. Is Councilmember Mamaux precluded from participating in
other decisions concerning the comrnunity facilities 
district?
CONCLUS IONS
1. we conf irrn that councilnernber Mamaux must disqualify
hirnself from participating j,n any decision which wiLl foreseeably
and materially affect his e:np1oyer, center Mortgage, or any
business entity that is a parent or subsidiary of center Mortgage
or othenrrise related to center llortgage, including center
Development and c & N Bank corp. In addition, Councilrnember
Manaux nust disqualify himself fron any decision whj,ch will
materially affect Mr. Dean Greenberg, the sole owner of the
business entities. 
It appears that any decisions concerning Phase
I, zone 5 or zone 25 rrill materially affect these economic
interests.
2. Since the decisions on Phase l, Zone 5 and Zone 25 will
not affect a significant segment of the jurisdiction 
i-n the sarne
nanner as they w111 affect the councilnember's f i-nancial
interests, the I'public generalLy" exception wil,l- not perrnit the
councilmember to participate in decisions for whj-ch he is
otherwise disqualif ied.
3. 
Ho$rever, independent decisions concerning the annexation
of other zones is perrnitted provided the councilmember has no
other econonic interest in the decisions and the decisions are
separable from those concerning Phase I, Zone 5 and Zone 25.
FACTS
The facts are substantiatly the same as those set forth j.n
our previous advj-ce letter to you.
The carLsbad city Council $/ilL be considering
the adoption of a resolution of intention to create
a llello-Roos Cornrnunity Facilj.ties District in
carlsbad. The purpose of the !'1e1lo-Roos district
is to permit the city to seI1 bonds for the
construction of public facilities 
in the district,
including a library, city haII, regional park,
roads and freeway interchanges. Construction of
such facilities 
is required prior to the
development of the land in the area. The bonds
wi1l be paid from a special tax gssessed on the
property in the district.
The proposed tax would be collected annualJ.y
with a final lump sum tax payment required at the

time the landowner obtains a building permit' 
The
proposed per acre tax for property zoned Iow
'aE"'=itv risidential 
would range fron 9227 to $23o
p"r ""ie. 
For cornnercial' proPerty, the tax woul-d
iange from $2,0oo to S3,o00 per acre per-year'
onc5 a buil-ding perrnit fras been obtained and the
final fee is piia, the Property will no longer be
taxed bY the Me1lo-Roos district'

center ltortgage. center !'tortgage is owned by c & N
Bank corP., -wtrich in turn is sole1y owned by Dean
ereenberg. Dean Greenberg also owns center
Developm6nt. Center Development owns apProxinately
225 acies of property which wifl be subject to the
special tax ii the resolution to adopt the
clrnrnunity facilities 
district is accepted'

incruaea in your ietter and through telephone conversations n ith
rinurr"" Di-reltor James E1]iot has been considered in this letter.

council will- consider the first 
phase as a whole' Phase
I includes 12 zones and encompasses 40? of the entire city'

owns 2i6 acres in zone s under the nane rrcarlsbad

than 1OO acres in Phase I.
Phase II involves the future annexation of the remaining
,o.,..- i.,io the district, 
including zone 25' The annexation will

to the cj'ty council on. a- zone-by-zone basis' - center
Devilopruent owns approxlnateJ-y one-ha1f-of the undeveloped
pi"p"tly in Zone 2-5- under the nane rtCarlsbad Professional'rr zorle

If the district 
is approved, only the undeveloped properties
in the phase I area will LL assessed, developed property in-Phase
i will not be affected. 
The actual property assessment wiJ'1 vary
depending on the zoning of the property. in the Phase I area'

uses .

tax rates. 
Thus, even $/here properties' characteristics are
identical, the property will -Ue Laxea differentLy depending on the
area in which the ProPertY is located'

ANALYSIS
1. The Mello-Roos communitv Facilities District
This letter confirms the advice provided' to you on JuIy 6,
1990. As l^re stated in that letter, since Councilmernber Mamaux is
employed hrith center Mortgage, center Mortgage is an economic
interest of the councilnenber. (section 871o3(d).) Further,
since center Mortgage is whoIly ovrned by c & N Bank corp, and c &
N Bank corp is wholly owned by Mr. Dean Greenberg, both C & N Bank
Corp and Mr. creenberg are also treated as sources of i-ncome to
councilmember Mamaux. (Regulation L8706i Regulation 18236(a) i
Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069. ) Fj.na11y, since Mr.
creenberg is the controlling owner of both Center Mortgage and
center Development, Center Development is also an rrotherwise
related business entityrt as defined in the Act. 
(Regulation
18236(b).) Thus, center Development is also a source of i.ncone to
Councilmember Mamaux for purposes of the conf l ict-of- interest
provisions of the Act.
consequently, the counciLmernber nay not participate in any
decision that will have a reasonablv foreseeabl,e and material
financial effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public
generally, on Center t{ortgage, C & N Bank Corp, center Development
or ttr. Greenberg personal,ly as sole owner of the business entity
that is the source of incone to the councilmember.
center Development owns property within the proposed Mello-
Roos district 
in Phase I and Phase II, and therefore will be
foreseeably affected by the decisions on Phase f of the Mello-Roos
District and Zones 5 and 25. Ho$/ever, we couLd not determine the
magnitude of the financial effect on Mr. Greenberg or
Ur. creenberg's businesses caused by the creation of the
assessment district, 
thus, we provided the appropriate reguJ.ations
and left the factual deterrnination of the rnagnitude of fj-nancial
effect to you. { /
2- Public Generallv: Phase I Inclusive of Zone 5
Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision nay participate in the decision
't I In your request for follow up advi,ce you stated with respect
to the magnitude of the financial effect that you did not believe
it would be substantial,. You stated that any value added by the
planned faciLities 
and earlier development of the Property !'ould
te offset by the tax burden assessed on each property. The
Cornmission cannot evaluate the factua] question of whether there
will be a rnaterial financial effect cin the council'nember' s
economic interest. 
If you conclude that the effect on the
councilmenber's economic interests will not neet any of the
thresholds set forth in our previous letter, the council-member
would not have a conf li.ct of interest and nay participate in the
decisions.


Our File No. I-9O-543
Page 5
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generalLy. For the [public genera]ly'r excep-
tion to apply, a decision must affect the official-'s interests in
substantiallv the same manner as it woul-d affect a siqnificant
sedment of the public. (Regulation 187o3, copy enclosed.)
The rtpublicrr consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
asency in question. (Tn re Osren (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copy
enclosed.) This is so because all the residents of the jurisdic-
tion are constituents of the official. 
Thus, the councilmember
may participate in the Me1lo-Roos District decisions only if the
effect on the councilmenber's financial interest is substantiallv
the same as the effect on a siqnificant segrment of the population
of the ci.ty of carlsbad.
You afso stated that of the various persons which are
economj.c interests of the councilmember, only center Development
and Mr. Greenberg will be foreseeably affected by the decision
because center Developnent ovrns property in one of the proposed
zones and lIr. Greenberg owns Center development. Thus, for the
public Aenerally exception to apply, the decision will have to
affect a significant seg-ment of the public in the sane nanner as
it would affect center Development and Mr. Greenberg.
You stated that the first 
phase of the project encompasses
4ot of the entire city. 
However, it does not appear that all
those Dersons in the Phase I area will affected in the same Inanner
as center Devel,opnent. Despite the fact that the first phase of
the district 
will be deaLt with as a whole by the city council,
the fact that each zone will have different standards and each
type of use in the zones a different assessment suggests that the
number of persons affected in the same manner as Center
Development is relatively sma1l.
For example, the decisions concerning the district will
affect only the undeveloped properties in the Phase I area. This
neans that all developed property in Phase I will not be affected
in the same manner as Center Developnent's property which is
undeveloped. Further, since the assessment will vary depending on
the zoning of the property in the Phase I area, of the properties
in Phase I that will be affected only those that are zoned
industrial or comrnercial wi,I1 be affected in the same nanner as
center Development's property.5/ This further reduces those
persons in Phase I who wj.lL be siurilarly affected.
Two other factors reduce the number of persons that will be
affected in the same rnanner as center Developnent. Phase I is
property in the
An additionaL
5/ Approxirnately
district is zoned
commercial.
11? of the
industrial.
first phase of the
38 is zoned


Our Fi■e No.
Page 6
I-90-543
divided into two areas $/ith different tax rates. 
Thus, even where
a property's characteristics are identical to center
Development's, the property.will be taxed differently if it is
Located in the other area.5/ Finally, since the size of center
Development's property holdings is substantial, and theoretically
only persons owning approximately the same amount of property as
center Development would be affected in the same manner, the
nunber constituting the segrment sirnilarly affected is even further
reduced and siould not appear to constitute a significant segment
as intended by the exception. // 
(fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC ops.
1, copy enclosed. )
In taking all these factors into consideration, it does not
appear that a significant segment of carlsbad wil-I be affected in
substantially the same manner as the councifrnember. Thus, the
public generally exceptj,on would not appIy.
As for decisions specifically concernj,ng Zones 5 and 25,8/ it
appears that the number of landoe/ners in the respective zones is
fli too small to constitute a significant segment, even if those
landowners were similarly situated which, as discussed above, they
are probably not.
3. Secrmentation of Decisions
rn the initial 
letter the guestions pertained to the
councilmember's participation in the decision to adopt the city
wide district 
which included property owned by an econonic
interest. 
In your follow up letter, you have expressed concern
about other city council decisions concerning the district.
Generally, every governmental decision nust be analyzed
independently with respect to the foreseeability and the material-
ity of a financial effect on an official's 
economic interest. (In
re o$ren (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77r copY enclosed.) However, under
some circunstances a series of decisions rnay be too interrelated
to be considered separately. (Mi11er Advice Letter, No. A-82-119,
copy enclosed.) For example, if it is deterrnined that an official
hal a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific
project because of the project's inpact on an econornic interest,
the official 
nay be similarly disqualified as to decisions
6/ 
zone 5 is in Area 1, rrhich will be subject to a higher
assesslnent.
' t 
when you consider that center Developnent owns 236 acres which
will be subject to the assessnent and onl,y 12 other property
owners in the Phase I area own nore than 2oo acres that $/il-I be
the subject to the assessment, the sgqnent similarly affected is a
much srnaller segTment than it vrould at first 
appear.
8/ 
zone 5 includes 34 property oe/ners, zone 25 includes only four
property olrners.


our File No.
Page 7
I-90-543
concerning the f inancj.ng of that project. 
The reason is that the
decisions concerning the financing of the project could in fact
alter the previous decision for whj-ch the officiaL 
r.ras
disgualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-o38, copy enclosed.)
conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as smaller
projects in a larger project, and none affects the decisions on
the other projects, each project roay be analyzed separately to
determine if the official 
has a conflict of interest. 
(Kilian
Advice Letter, No. A-89-522, copy enclosed.)
You stated in your letter that Center Mortgage has property
in Zone 5 and zone 25. It appears from the information you
provided that the decisions on Phase r of the project, which
incLudes Zone 5, would be considered as a who1e. Thus, decisions
concerning Phase I are inseparable from decisions concerning Zone
5 for which the councilnenber appears to have a conflict of
interest. 
Ilolrever, independent decisions concerning the
annexation of other zones (other than zone 25) mj,ght be
separable.9/ It appears that the later annexatj.on of other Phase
IL zones will not foreseeably affect the councilnember's financial
interests .
If indeed the decisions are separabl,e and the councilrnernber
determj-nes he has a conflict of interest wj.th respect to some, but
not all the decisions and various zones, the following procedure
shoul-d be followed to permit the counciLnember to participate:
(1) The decisions for whj.ch the councilrnember has a
dJ.squalifying financial interest should be segregated fron the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the councilnember is
disqualified should be considered first, 
and a final decision
reached by the city council without the councilmember
participating in any way.
(3) once a decision has been nade on the projects for which
the councilmember has a disqual-ifying financial interest, he nay
participate in the deliberations regarding the other zones, so
long as those delj.berations do not result in a reopeningT or in any
sray affect the decisions from uihich he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-85-343, copy enclosed.)
9/ of course this deterroination urust, be made on a decision-by-
decision basis to ensure that the decisions in which the
councilnenber is pernitted to participate do not involve
independent conflicts of interest or in any way influence
decisions for which he was disgualified.


Our Fi■e No.  
■-90-543
Page 8
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
John w.
counsel ,

in the same manner as Center Developnent's property which is
undeveloped. Further, since the assessment will vary depending on
the zoning of the property in the Phase I area, of the properties
in Phase I that will be affected only those that are zoned
industrial or comrnercial wi,I1 be affected in the same nanner as
center Development's property.5/ This further reduces those
persons in Phase I who wj.lL be siurilarly affected.
Two other factors reduce the number of persons that will be
affected in the same rnanner as center Developnent. Phase I is
property in the

district is zoned
commercial.

industrial.
first phase of the

divided into two areas $/ith different tax rates. 
Thus, even where
a property's characteristics are identical to center
Development's, the property.will be taxed differently if it is
Located in the other area.5/ Finally, since the size of center
Development's property holdings is substantial, and theoretically
only persons owning approximately the same amount of property as
center Development would be affected in the same manner, the
nunber constituting the segrment sirnilarly affected is even further
reduced and siould not appear to constitute a significant segment
as intended by the exception. // 
(fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC ops.
1, copy enclosed. )
In taking all these factors into consideration, it does not
appear that a significant segment of carlsbad wil-I be affected in
substantially the same manner as the councifrnember. Thus, the
public generally exceptj,on would not appIy.
As for decisions specifically concernj,ng Zones 5 and 25,8/ it
appears that the number of landoe/ners in the respective zones is
fli too small to constitute a significant segment, even if those
landowners were similarly situated which, as discussed above, they
are probably not.
3. Secrmentation of Decisions
rn the initial 
letter the guestions pertained to the
councilmember's participation in the decision to adopt the city
wide district 
which included property owned by an econonic
interest. 
In your follow up letter, you have expressed concern
about other city council decisions concerning the district.
Generally, every governmental decision nust be analyzed
independently with respect to the foreseeability and the material-
ity of a financial effect on an official's 
economic interest. (In
re o$ren (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77r copY enclosed.) However, under
some circunstances a series of decisions rnay be too interrelated
to be considered separately. (Mi11er Advice Letter, No. A-82-119,
copy enclosed.) For example, if it is deterrnined that an official
hal a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific
project because of the project's inpact on an econornic interest,
the official 
nay be similarly disqualified as to decisions
6/

zone 5 includes 34 property oe/ners, zone 25 includes only four

our concerning the f inancj.ng of that project. 
The reason is that the
decisions concerning the financing of the project could in fact
alter the previous decision for whj-ch the officiaL 
r.ras
disgualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-o38, copy enclosed.)
conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as smaller
projects in a larger project, and none affects the decisions on
the other projects, each project roay be analyzed separately to
determine if the official 
has a conflict of interest. 
(Kilian
Advice Letter, No. A-89-522, copy enclosed.)
You stated in your letter that Center Mortgage has property
in Zone 5 and zone 25. It appears from the information you
provided that the decisions on Phase r of the project, which
incLudes Zone 5, would be considered as a who1e. Thus, decisions
concerning Phase I are inseparable from decisions concerning Zone
5 for which the councilnenber appears to have a conflict of
interest. 
Ilolrever, independent decisions concerning the
annexation of other zones (other than zone 25) mj,ght be
separable.9/ It appears that the later annexatj.on of other Phase
IL zones will not foreseeably affect the councilnember's financial
interests .
If indeed the decisions are separabl,e and the councilrnernber
determj-nes he has a conflict of interest wj.th respect to some, but
not all the decisions and various zones, the following procedure
shoul-d be followed to permit the counciLnember to participate:
(1) The decisions for whj.ch the councilrnember has a
dJ.squalifying financial interest should be segregated fron the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the councilnember is
disqualified should be considered first, 
and a final decision
reached by the city council without the councilmember
participating in any way.
(3) once a decision has been nade on the projects for which
the councilmember has a disqual-ifying financial interest, he nay
participate in the deliberations regarding the other zones, so
long as those delj.berations do not result in a reopeningT or in any
sray affect the decisions from uihich he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-85-343, copy enclosed.)
9/ of course this deterroination urust, be made on a decision-by-
decision basis to ensure that the decisions in which the
councilnenber is pernitted to participate do not involve
independent conflicts of interest or in any way influence
decisions for which he was disgualified.
